records.title: Memory-Nonlinearity Trade-off across Quantum Reservoir Computing Frameworks
records.citation: Saud Čindrak, Lara Giebeler, Niclas Götting, Christopher Gies, Kathy Lüdge (2026). Memory-Nonlinearity Trade-off across Quantum Reservoir Computing Frameworks. arXiv:2603.21371. https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.21371
assumptions: QRC performance is governed by a tunable memory-nonlinearity trade-off measurable via IPC decomposition.
Hamiltonian and measured observables constrain effective degrees of freedom more than encoding choice.
Task suitability is benchmark-dependent; Lorenz and Mackey-Glass are used as stress tests for nonlinearity and memory.
contributions: Unifies memory restriction, weak measurement, edge-of-chaos tuning, and dissipative Lindblad QRC under one memory-nonlinearity framework.
Provides cross-framework IPC analysis and identifies parameter sweet spots where total IPC and prediction improve over baseline protocol.
Connects physical operating regimes (Ising transverse field and dissipation rate) to computational capacity trade-offs.
claims: Distinct QRC protocols exhibit the same qualitative memory-nonlinearity trade-off curve.
Intermediate operating regimes can outperform fully reset baselines on total IPC and prediction error.
Near localization-ergodicity transition and moderate dissipation, nonlinear capacity peaks while retaining usable memory.
conclusions: Memory-nonlinearity balance is a central design principle for QRC architecture selection and tuning.
Protocol-specific knobs (reset length, measurement strength, transverse field, dissipation) are practical controls for feature-map behavior.
limitations: Evidence focuses on benchmark time-series tasks; transfer to image-classification settings requires targeted validation.
Reported sweet spots are architecture- and task-dependent and need re-identification under changed observables/encodings.
future_work: Apply the unified framework to more advanced architectures and hardware-constrained implementations.
Extend IPC-guided design to additional datasets and richer measurement/readout configurations.
key_equations: dx/dt = σ (y - x), dy/dt = x(ρ - z) - y, dz/dt = xy - βz (Lorenz-63; σ=10, ρ=28, β=8/3).
dx/dt = β x(t-τ_MG)/(1 + x(t-τ_MG)^10) - γ x(t) (Mackey-Glass benchmark dynamic).
IPC_tot = IPC_1 + IPC_{≥2}, where IPC_1 tracks linear memory and IPC_{≥2} tracks nonlinear response.
notation_seeds: IPC_1 (linear memory), IPC_{≥2} (nonlinear capacity), IPC_tot (total capacity).
Reservoir control knobs: reset length r, measurement strength ϑ, transverse field h, dissipation rate γ.

evidence_atoms.content: Multiple QRC variants are governed by a shared memory-nonlinearity trade-off.
Trade-off is quantified using IPC decomposition and compared over Lorenz and Mackey-Glass tasks.
Lorenz benchmark dynamics: dx/dt = σ(y-x), dy/dt = x(ρ-z)-y, dz/dt = xy-βz with σ=10, ρ=28, β=8/3.
Near h≈0.5 (localization-ergodicity transition), nonlinear response peaks while memory decreases, increasing total IPC.
Performance analysis is benchmark-driven and may not transfer directly to other data modalities without re-tuning.

records.title: Real-time computing without stable states: A new framework for neural computation based on perturbations
assumptions: High-dimensional transient dynamics can be linearly decoded.
claims: Untrained recurrent dynamics with linear readout can solve temporal tasks.
conclusions: Reservoir dynamics can separate temporal features for readout.
limitations: Predates NISQ quantum noise and shot constraints.
key_equations: y_k(t)=sum_i w_ki x_i(t)

records.title: The Echo State Approach to Analysing and Training Recurrent Neural Networks
assumptions: Echo-state property ensures input-driven state stability.
contributions: Canonical ESN formulation for baseline parity.
claims: Linear readout training over fixed recurrent core is effective.
conclusions: Echo-state conditions are central to stable RC behavior.
limitations: Classical-only assumptions; no quantum feature map analysis.
